title: Chapter 6 — Fixing the File with the Plugin API
CHAPTER 6 —
FIXING THE FILE WITH THE PLUGIN API
segments.body_html:
The Figma REST API is a query interface. It answers questions about a file's current state. The Plugin API is the editor

Chapter 6 — Fixing the File with the Plu — Figma drew this line deliberately. When a script modifies a file through the REST API, nothing about the execution conte

beats.text: The Figma REST API is a query interface. It answers questions about a file's current state. The Plugin API is the editor interface , it operates on the file with the full authority of the editor itself, meaning changes appear immediately on the canvas and propagate to every other editor in the file via Figma's multiplayer sync. Figma drew this line deliberately. When a script modifies a file through the REST API, nothing about the execution context ensures a human is present or paying attention.

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

title: THE PLUGIN
API RUNTIME.
segments.body_html:
The Plugin API runs in a QuickJS WebAssembly sandbox inside the Figma desktop application. The desktop app is required —

The Plugin API Runtime — What this sandbox can do: read and write node properties (name, fills, strokes, opacity, visible, locked, effects, layou

beats.text: The Plugin API runs in a QuickJS WebAssembly sandbox inside the Figma desktop application. The desktop app is required , the plugin sandbox is not available in the browser editor. What this sandbox can do: read and write node properties (name, fills, strokes, opacity, visible, locked, effects, layout constraints); read and write variable names, descriptions, values, and collection structure; read and write component descriptions; traverse the full document tree; create, delete, and move nodes.

segments.label: 02 / 13 · THE PLUGIN API RUNTIME

title: THE STAGED
WORKFLOW.
segments.body_html: Phase 1 — Load:→Phase 2 — Preview:→Phase 3 — Apply:

beats.text: The fix plugin follows a strict three-phase sequence. No write happens until Phase 3, and Phase 3 requires explicit human confirmation. Phase 1 , Load: The user pastes the audit-report.json produced by figma-audit.js. The UI parses it and generates fix proposals for every finding that can be automatically addressed. Findings that require design judgment are excluded , flagged for manual review, not proposed for automation.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · THE STAGED WORKFLOW

title: LOADING THE
PLUGIN.
segments.body_html:
During development: open Figma Desktop, go to Plugins → Development → Import plugin from manifest, navigate to figma-fix

Loading the Plugin — For team distribution, publish to your organization's private plugin library through the Figma admin panel.

beats.text: During development: open Figma Desktop, go to Plugins goes to Development goes to Import plugin from manifest, navigate to figma-fix-plugin/manifest.json. Run it from Plugins goes to Development goes to Figma Fix. For team distribution, publish to your organization's private plugin library through the Figma admin panel.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · LOADING THE PLUGIN

segments.section: WHAT THE APPROVAL GATE IS FOR
title: WHAT THE APPROVAL GATE
IS FOR.
segments.body_html:
NAME001 — Variable name violates the three-tier naming convention (wrong depth, invalid characters, unknown category)
TOK001 — Alias references a deleted variable ID
TOK002 — Semantic token has no description field
COMP001 — Component has no description field

beats.text: The approval gate is not a UX nicety. It is where two things happen that the tool cannot substitute for. The first is judgment about semantic meaning. The audit can tell you that a variable is named Color 4, which violates the naming convention from Chapter 4. It cannot tell you whether Color 4 should become color/primitive/blue-500 or color/semantic/action-primary , those are different claims about what the color means in the design system. One of them may be correct.

segments.label: 06 / 13 · WHAT THE APPROVAL GATE IS FOR

title: WHAT TO
NEVER AUTOMATE.
segments.body_html: Alias target changes.→Value changes.→Component restructuring.→Deletions.

beats.text: Structural fixes , description text, a description where there was none , are safe to automate because the only question is "does this field have content?" The answer is deterministic. The following are not safe to automate, regardless of how confident the audit findings look:

segments.label: 07 / 13 · WHAT TO NEVER AUTOMATE

title: THE BACKUP
PATTERN.
segments.body_html:
Before any bulk fix run, create a version history checkpoint in Figma. File menu → Save to Version History → add a note

The Backup Pattern — If your plan does not include version history: File → Save local copy to export the file as .fig. This is a manual snaps

beats.text: Before any bulk fix run, create a version history checkpoint in Figma. File menu goes to Save to Version History goes to add a note like "Pre-audit-fix 2026-06-01." If your plan does not include version history: File goes to Save local copy to export the file as .fig. This is a manual snapshot, not a live backup, but it is a restore point.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · THE BACKUP PATTERN

title: FAILURE MODES
segments.body_html:
10%
of proposed fixes fail with this error | The fixture was cre
→
10%
of proposed fixes report this error, stop the run — re-audit

beats.text: Understanding how this plugin fails is as important as understanding how it works. The ID mismatch. The audit report captures node IDs at the time the fixture was created. If the file was modified between fixture creation and plugin execution, some IDs may have changed or been deleted. The Variable not found by ID error in the failed results is the signal. If more than 10 percent of proposed fixes report this error, stop the run , re-audit against a fresh fixture before continuing.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · FAILURE MODES

title: WHAT COMES
NEXT.
segments.body_html: The plugin gives you the write path. The audit gives you the findings. What you do not yet have is a way to run this loo

beats.text: The plugin gives you the write path. The audit gives you the findings. What you do not yet have is a way to run this loop continuously , not as a one-time remediation effort, but as an ongoing check that fires whenever the file changes. Chapter 7 covers webhooks and event-driven automation: how Figma notifies external systems when a file is updated, and how to use those notifications to trigger the audit pipeline automatically so that the 247 errors never accumulate in the first place.

segments.label: 10 / 13 · WHAT COMES NEXT

title: FIGMA-TOOLS:
PLUGIN SAFETY RULE
segments.body_html:
·Any write operation outside the APPLY_FIXES handler = a safety regression. Remove it
·Auto-generated descriptions in proposedValue() = replace with the placeholder from the
·Absent confirm() dialog = add it before any plugin use on a real file.
·Fatigue-gate risk = file as a known limitation in README.md and consider adding a

beats.text: figma-fix-plugin NEVER writes to the Figma canvas without explicit human approval. All write operations require the user to: (1) review proposed changes in the Phase 2 preview, (2) approve each change individually or confirm "Approve All," and (3) confirm the apply dialog. The plugin performs reads and previews only by default. Do not add any auto-apply logic to code.js. Do not add NAME001 to AUTO_FIXABLE_RULES. This rule exists because Figma does not support batch undo for plugin operations.

segments.label: 11 / 13 · FIGMA-TOOLS: PLUGIN SAFETY RUL
